target.title: fix(acpx): scrub provider credential env from ACP harness spawns
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `sessions_spawn` with `runtime:"acp"` + `agentId:"claude"` fails immediately with `AcpRuntimeError: Internal error: Invalid API key · code=ACP_TURN_FAILED`.
- **Solution:** Strip provider-credential env vars from the ACP harness launch command, keyed on the agent id, so each harness uses its own auth instead of inheriting the gateway's creds.
- **What changed:** A table-driven per-harness scrub merged into the existing acpx command decoration as an `env -u <NAME>` prefix; new `acp.scrubProviderEnv` config knob (default `true`).
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** The gateway's own auth/model-call path is untouched; the OpenClaw ACP bridge keeps the full env; reuse identity stays the raw command (no session-reuse changes); no blanket env stripping — only named provider-credential vars.

## Motivation

Operators set `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in `~/.openclaw/.env` so the gateway can authenticate its own model calls. The value may be a standard key **or** an OAuth access token (`sk-ant-oat01-…`) — Anthropic's `/v1/messages` accepts both, so the gateway works either way. But Claude Code's CLI auth path **rejects** an OAuth token supplied via `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` because it expects its own Keychain OAuth flow. Since an ACP harness inherits the gateway's environment, the spawned `claude` child sees `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, tries to use it, and dies with "Invalid API key" before doing any work.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** ACP spawn of `agentId:"claude"` fails with `Invalid API key`.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS; Claude Code authenticated via Keychain OAuth; `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` present in `~/.openclaw/.env`. The reporter confirmed the diagnosis on this setup (see below). **Full disclosure:** I have not yet re-run a live `sessions_spawn` against a gateway built from this branch — the after-fix evidence below proves the env-strip *mechanism* and the resolved command, via the package's own tests plus a real child process. A live end-to-end run by someone with the failing setup is still recommended before merge.
- **Exact steps/commands run after this patch:**
  - `pnpm` acpx suite: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-acpx.config.ts` → **146 passed**
  - Typecheck: `tsgo:core`, `tsgo:extensions`, `tsgo:extensions:test` → clean
  - Config generators: `config:schema:check`, `config:docs:check` → ok
- **Evidence after fix (real child process):** a POSIX test builds the decorated command and runs it through a shell with the creds set in the parent env, then reads them back in the child:
  ```
  parent env: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-oat01-…, ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN=…, OPENCLAW_KEEPME=still-here
  command:    env -u ANTHROPIC_API_KEY -u ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN node -e '…print the three vars…'
  child sees: { a: null, t: null, keep: "still-here" }
  ```
  i.e. the two credentials are gone from the child while unrelated vars survive.
- **Observed result after fix:** harness launch command for `claude` becomes `env -u ANTHROPIC_API_KEY -u ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN … node ".../claude-agent-acp-wrapper.mjs" …`; the child no longer inherits the colliding credential.
- **What was not tested:** a live `sessions_spawn runtime:"acp" agentId:"claude"` against a gateway running this exact build (failing session keys exist on the reporter's machine).
- **Before evidence:** reporter's repro — `claude --print 'ping'` fails (inherits `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`); `env -u ANTHROPIC_API_KEY claude --print 'ping'` works. Renaming the var to `OPENCLAW_ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in `.env` also fixes ACP spawns, confirming the collision is the cause.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause:** Auth-path collision. The gateway's `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` (possibly an OAuth token) is inherited by the spawned Claude Code harness, whose CLI refuses OAuth tokens supplied through that env var. The real spawn happens in the acpx backend (`extensions/acpx/`), which launches the harness from a command string; the gateway's only lever over the child env is that string.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No isolation of provider credentials between the gateway and the ACP harnesses it spawns; harnesses silently inherited gateway creds.
- **Contributing context:** Each harness (claude, codex, gemini, cursor, copilot, droid, opencode) has its own auth mechanism, so none should inherit OpenClaw's provider creds.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file:** `extensions/acpx/src/provider-env-scrub.test.ts`, `extensions/acpx/src/process-lease.test.ts` (incl. real child-process strip), `extensions/acpx/src/runtime.provider-env-scrub.test.ts` (scoped-registry wiring + classification).
- **Scenario the test should lock in:** resolving the `claude` harness command yields an `env -u ANTHROPIC_API_KEY -u ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN` prefix; codex/gemini strip their providers; the openclaw bridge is untouched; `acp.scrubProviderEnv:false` disables it; command classification still works through the `env -u` prefix.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** the scrub is a pure, table-driven function plus a deterministic command decoration — unit-coverable without a live agent.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- ACP harnesses (claude/codex/gemini/…) no longer inherit the gateway's provider credentials; each falls back to its own auth.
- New config `acp.scrubProviderEnv` (boolean, **default `true`**). Set `false` to restore the old inherit-everything behavior.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
sessions_spawn(acp, claude) -> spawn "node claude-agent-acp-wrapper.mjs"
  child env INHERITS ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (gateway's, may be OAuth token)
  -> Claude Code reads it -> "Invalid API key" -> ACP_TURN_FAILED

After:
sessions_spawn(acp, claude) -> spawn "env -u ANTHROPIC_API_KEY -u ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN node claude-agent-acp-wrapper.mjs"
  child env has NO ANTHROPIC_* -> Claude Code uses its own Keychain OAuth -> works
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **Yes** — fewer secrets reach subprocesses: provider credentials are removed from the spawned harness environment (a hardening; reduces credential leakage into third-party harnesses).
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **Yes (minor)** — the harness command string gains a leading `env -u <NAME>` prefix. Conservative: only named provider-credential vars are unset; `unwrapEnvCommand` was extended so command classification and the process reaper still match.
- Data access scope changed? **No**
- Risk + mitigation: see Risks below.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw gateway + acpx backend
- Model/provider: Anthropic (Claude Code harness over ACP)
- Integration/channel: ACP `sessions_spawn`
- Relevant config (redacted): `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` set in `~/.openclaw/.env` (value is an OAuth token in the reporter's case)

### Steps

1. With `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` set in `~/.openclaw/.env`, run `sessions_spawn` with `runtime:"acp"`, `agentId:"claude"`.
2. Observe failure (before this patch).
3. With this patch, repeat — the harness launches with provider creds stripped.

### Expected

- The Claude Code harness starts and uses its own Keychain OAuth.

### Actual (before fix)

- `AcpRuntimeError: Internal error: Invalid API key · code=ACP_TURN_FAILED`.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (TDD: tests written red, then green; see test files)
- [x] Trace/log snippets (child-process strip output above)
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** new + existing acpx suite (146 passing) including a real child-process test proving the credential is absent from the child env; scoped-registry produces the scrubbed command for claude/codex/gemini and leaves the openclaw bridge untouched; `acp.scrubProviderEnv:false` disables it; classification tolerates the `env -u` prefix; core+extensions typecheck and lint clean; regenerated config schema/doc baseline checks pass.
- **Edge cases checked:** unknown harness id (strips the union of known provider creds); Windows no-op (`env -u` not portable); de-duped credential names; persistent-session reuse unaffected (reuse identity is the raw command).
- **What I did NOT verify:** a live `sessions_spawn runtime:"acp" agentId:"claude"` against a gateway built from this branch.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Mostly** — harness child env changes by design (the fix). Any setup that *relied* on a harness inheriting a gateway provider cred can restore the old behavior with `acp.scrubProviderEnv: false`.
- Config/env changes? **Yes** — new optional `acp.scrubProviderEnv` (default `true`).
- Migration needed? **No.**

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** A harness that legitimately depended on inheriting a gateway provider credential loses it.
  - **Mitigation:** Per-harness table is conservative (only that harness's own provider creds for known harnesses); the `openclaw` bridge is exempt; global opt-out via `acp.scrubProviderEnv: false`.
- **Risk:** The `env -u` prefix could confuse command classification / process cleanup.
  - **Mitigation:** `unwrapEnvCommand` extended to skip `-u NAME`; covered by classification tests; reuse/cleanup use the raw stable command, which is unchanged.

target.title: feat(secrets): scan backup files for plaintext provider apiKey values
target.body:
## Summary

Adds backup file secret scanning to the `openclaw secrets audit` command. This addresses a security gap identified in issue #11829 where old backup files (e.g., `models.json.20260501.bak`, `openclaw.json.old`) may retain plaintext provider API keys even after the active config is sanitized.

## Changes

- **storage-scan.ts**: New `listKnownSecretFileBackups()` function that discovers backup files in the config directory and agents directory matching patterns like `models.json.*`, `openclaw.json.*`, `*.bak`, `*.backup`, `*.old`
- **audit.ts**: New `collectBackupResidue()` function that scans backup files for plaintext `apiKey` values and reports them as `LEGACY_RESIDUE` findings
- **audit.test.ts**: 3 test cases covering:
  - Detecting plaintext apiKey in `.bak` files
  - Detecting plaintext apiKey in `.old` files  
  - Not flagging non-secret markers (env var names) in backup files

Part of #11829 - Protecting API Keys from Agent Access

target.title: feat(subagents): add execution backend placement contract
target.body:
## Summary

Adds the first real implementation slice for #82017: `sessions_spawn` now accepts an optional execution placement request and validates it against `agents.executionBackends`.

This PR keeps runtime behavior conservative: the built-in `local` `process` backend is the only executable backend in this release. Forward-looking `container` and `kubernetes` configs are accepted by config validation, but spawn rejects them with an explicit runtime-policy error until those backends are implemented. Placement is recorded on spawned run metadata/list output for readback; non-local runner dispatch is intentionally not implemented here.

## Related PRs

- Runtime self-context / runtime tool companion: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/90101

## CONTRIBUTING alignment

- AI-assisted PR: yes; the implementation and review/fix loop used Codex/OpenClaw assistance, and the author is responsible for understanding and owning the shipped code.
- Current-head real behavior proof: included below for `ae6a3f7fdfb1`, covering default local process placement, configured local profile placement, and unsupported Kubernetes fail-closed behavior.
- Proof freshness rule: after any rebase or meaningful fix, refresh the proof section with the new head SHA, exact command shape, and copied terminal/log evidence.
- Bot review status: ClawSweeper has marked contributor proof sufficient and ready for maintainer look; the remaining action is maintainer approval of the public contract.
- Takeover readiness: maintainer edits are enabled, related PR #90101 is linked, and this PR intentionally does not edit `CHANGELOG.md`.

## Maintainer contract decision requested

Please explicitly choose one before merge:

- Approve the v1 additive contract: `agents.executionBackends` plus `sessions_spawn.execution`, with only local `process` execution implemented in this slice and `container`/`kubernetes` configs accepted but rejected fail-closed at spawn time.
- Narrow before merge: restrict the schema/docs to local `process` only until non-local backend semantics are fully designed.
- Pause for broader design: hold this PR until runtime self-context, execution placement, and remote worker protocol decisions are reviewed together.

## Linked issue

Related to #82017.

## What changed

- Added `agents.executionBackends` config schema/types/help/labels/tags/docs.
- Added `sessions_spawn.execution.backend/profile` schema and plumbing.
- Added placement resolution for subagent and ACP spawns.
- Persisted execution placement into subagent run records and list output.
- Regenerated prompt snapshots for the new model-facing tool schema.
- Added unit coverage for config validation, placement resolution, tool parameter forwarding, and list readback.

## Behavior

- Omitted execution request defaults to `{ backend: "local", type: "process" }`.
- Configured local process profiles can be requested by name, validated, and recorded on the run.
- Unknown backends/profiles fail before spawn starts.
- `container`/`kubernetes` backends are schema-valid but rejected at spawn time for now.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: `sessions_spawn.execution` placement resolution accepts the default local process backend, accepts a configured local profile, and rejects an unsupported Kubernetes backend instead of silently falling back.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw repository checkout at branch `feat/execution-backends-local`, rebased on `origin/main`, running the patched TypeScript source with Node/tsx.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
node --import tsx <<'EOF'
import { resolveAgentExecutionPlacement } from './src/agents/execution-backends.ts';

const cfg = {
  agents: {
    executionBackends: {
      local: {
        type: 'process',
        profiles: {
          small: { resources: { requests: { cpu: '500m', memory: '1Gi' } } },
        },
      },
      k8s: {
        type: 'kubernetes',
        profiles: { 'large-build': {} },
      },
    },
  },
};

for (const [name, request] of [
  ['default local process', undefined],
  ['local small profile', { backend: 'local', profile: 'small' }],
  ['unsupported k8s backend', { backend: 'k8s', profile: 'large-build' }],
]) {
  console.log(`${name}: ${JSON.stringify(resolveAgentExecutionPlacement({ cfg, request }))}`);
}
EOF
```

- Evidence after fix (copied live terminal output):

```text
default local process: {"ok":true,"execution":{"backend":"local","type":"process"}}
local small profile: {"ok":true,"execution":{"backend":"local","type":"process","profile":"small"}}
unsupported k8s backend: {"ok":false,"error":"execution backend \"k8s\" has type \"kubernetes\", but only local process execution is supported in this release"}
```

- Observed result after fix: local process placement resolves successfully, the configured `small` profile is preserved in the placement result, and unsupported Kubernetes placement returns an explicit runtime-policy error.
- What was not tested: full non-local container/Kubernetes runner dispatch, because this PR intentionally does not implement non-local backend runners.

## Verification

Ran locally on branch `feat/execution-backends-local`:

- `pnpm tsgo:test:src`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/agents/execution-backends.ts src/agents/acp-spawn.ts src/agents/subagent-spawn.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.ts src/agents/subagent-list.ts src/agents/subagent-list.test.ts src/config/zod-schema.agents.ts src/config/types.agents.ts src/config/schema.tags.ts src/config/schema.labels.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=.vitest-include-help-quality node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.runtime-config.config.ts src/config/schema.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts src/agents/execution-backends.test.ts src/agents/subagent-list.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=.vitest-include-agents node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=.vitest-include-agents-tools node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts`
- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/gateway/config-agents.md docs/tools/subagents.md`
- `pnpm format:docs:check docs/gateway/config-agents.md docs/tools/subagents.md`
- `pnpm prompt:snapshots:check`

## Security impact

No new secrets, network access, or remote execution implementation. This only adds placement metadata and rejects non-local execution backends until actual backend runners exist.

target.title: Materialize node-host inline interpreter eval before exec approval
target.body:
## Summary
- Materialize exact direct node-host inline eval forms into deterministic scripts before system-run approval planning:
  - `python -c CODE`
  - `python3 -c CODE`
  - absolute/venv Python paths with `-c CODE`
  - `node -e CODE`
  - `node --eval CODE`
- Preserve the existing approval security model by rewriting argv to a real script file and letting `mutableFileOperand` hash binding apply.
- Preserve venv interpreter semantics by pinning materialized inline eval to the resolved executable path rather than the realpath target.
- Reuse the approved rewritten argv during dispatch when it matches the original `commandPreview`.
- Keep ambiguous or argv-sensitive inline eval shapes fail-closed instead of attempting a semantic rewrite.

## Security
Inline eval was previously denied because approval could not bind the code to a stable file artifact. This change does not allow raw inline eval. It writes the snippet to an OpenClaw-owned temp directory with deterministic sha256-based filenames and `0600` file mode, then plans approval against the generated script path. The original requested command remains visible through `commandPreview`.

Supported in this PR: exact three-argument direct Python `-c` and Node `-e`/`--eval` forms on non-Windows platforms.

Intentionally unsupported/fail-closed in this PR:
- Windows materialization.
- Shell wrappers such as `sh -c 'python -c "..."'`.
- Extra interpreter flags such as `python -B -c ...`.
- Trailing script arguments such as `python -c CODE arg` or `node --eval CODE arg`.
- Node eval-only shapes where rewriting changes semantics, such as `node --input-type=module --eval CODE`.
- Other eval forms such as `node -p`.

## Real Behavior Proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** Node-host `system.run.prepare` previously rejected direct inline interpreter eval because approval could not bind the inline code to a stable file. After the patch, exact direct inline eval forms are materialized into deterministic scripts, approval binds the generated script through `mutableFileOperand`, and argv-sensitive forms still fail closed.

**Real environment tested:** Live macOS node named `Mac mini video node`, OpenClaw `2026.5.18`, reached through the OpenClaw gateway after patching the installed node bundle with this PR's behavior.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `openclaw gateway call node.invoke -- system.run.prepare ...` and `openclaw gateway call node.invoke -- system.run ...` against the live Mac node for `/Users/ray/OpenClaw/venvs/video-editing/bin/python -c "import moviepy, cv2, pysubs2, srt, numpy; print(\"video stack ok\")"`. Also ran `openclaw gateway call node.invoke -- system.run.prepare ...` for `node --input-type=module --eval CODE` to verify the argv-sensitive Node form still fails closed.

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live output from the OpenClaw gateway. `system.run.prepare` accepts the venv Python inline probe and rewrites it to a generated script:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "command": "system.run.prepare",
  "payload": {
    "plan": {
      "argv": [
        "/Users/ray/OpenClaw/venvs/video-editing/bin/python",
        "/Users/ray/.openclaw/tmp/inline-eval/ba5c4c659710a40c81a09c008ae04c6a15b59726ab777fae1c87b270a8940648.py"
      ],
      "cwd": "/Users/ray/OpenClaw",
      "commandText": "/Users/ray/OpenClaw/venvs/video-editing/bin/python /Users/ray/.openclaw/tmp/inline-eval/ba5c4c659710a40c81a09c008ae04c6a15b59726ab777fae1c87b270a8940648.py",
      "commandPreview": "/Users/ray/OpenClaw/venvs/video-editing/bin/python -c \"import moviepy, cv2, pysubs2, srt, numpy; print(\\\"video stack ok\\\")\"",
      "mutableFileOperand": {
        "argvIndex": 1,
        "path": "/Users/ray/.openclaw/tmp/inline-eval/ba5c4c659710a40c81a09c008ae04c6a15b59726ab777fae1c87b270a8940648.py",
        "sha256": "058e59d6c5163d859f0d3ecb911cbb94688fe81b63e5630e124f86deda72f4d3"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

`system.run` on the same Mac node executes the inline probe successfully:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "command": "system.run",
  "payload": {
    "exitCode": 0,
    "timedOut": false,
    "success": true,
    "stdout": "video stack ok\n",
    "stderr": "",
    "error": null
  }
}
```

An argv-sensitive Node eval form still fails closed:

```text
Gateway call failed: GatewayClientRequestError: INVALID_REQUEST: SYSTEM_RUN_DENIED: approval cannot safely bind this interpreter/runtime command
```

That failure was for `node --input-type=module --eval CODE`, which should not be rewritten to `node --input-type=module file.js` because the semantics differ.

**Observed result after fix:** The live Mac node produced a plan whose argv preserved `/Users/ray/OpenClaw/venvs/video-editing/bin/python`, rewrote the inline code to `/Users/ray/.openclaw/tmp/inline-eval/ba5c4c659710a40c81a09c008ae04c6a15b59726ab777fae1c87b270a8940648.py`, and included `mutableFileOperand.sha256`. The same inline probe executed with `stdout` equal to `video stack ok\n`. The argv-sensitive Node form remained denied with `SYSTEM_RUN_DENIED`.

**What was not tested:** Windows materialization, shell-wrapper materialization, and semantic rewrites for eval forms with extra interpreter flags or trailing script argv remain intentionally unsupported in this PR.

## Tests
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/node-host/invoke-system-run-plan.test.ts src/node-host/invoke-system-run.test.ts --reporter=verbose` -> 2 files passed, 52 tests passed
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --pretty false --noEmit` -> passed
- `pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/node-host/invoke-system-run-plan.ts src/node-host/invoke-system-run-plan.test.ts src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts src/node-host/invoke-system-run.test.ts` -> 0 warnings, 0 errors
- `git diff --check` -> passed

target.title: memory: add local continuity snapshot helpers
target.body:
## Summary

Adds local-first helpers for reading and rendering recent continuity snapshots under `memory/recent`:

- parse frontmatter-based and legacy markdown continuity notes;
- render a structured recent snapshot document;
- build a small continuity manifest prioritized by status, priority, and recency;
- compare material continuity changes while ignoring TTL-only churn;
- expose the helpers through the focused `@openclaw/memory-host-sdk/runtime-files` surface.

This is intentionally only the local document/manifest layer. It does not add provider-backed extraction, command hooks, compaction hooks, or post-compaction behavior.

## Why

Agents currently have long-term memory files and session transcripts, but there is no small local-first continuity document format for the most recent active task state. This provides a narrow foundation that later integrations can use without mixing runtime hook behavior into this PR.

## Safety / scope

- Local filesystem only.
- No LLM/provider calls.
- No new hook registration.
- No command reply-path changes.
- No compaction/post-compaction behavior changes.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: The new local continuity helper can render a recent continuity snapshot, parse it back from disk, and build a prioritized continuity manifest that includes both the new `memory/recent/latest.md` file and an existing legacy markdown memory file.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, running this PR branch with Node/tsx against a temporary workspace under the macOS temp directory. No provider-backed extraction, no LLM call, no command hook, and no compaction hook were used.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Created a temporary workspace; wrote a rendered continuity snapshot to `memory/recent/latest.md` using `renderContinuitySnapshotMarkdown()`; wrote a legacy-style `memory/active-topics.md`; parsed the snapshot with `parseContinuityDocument()`; built a manifest with `buildContinuityManifest()`; printed it with `formatContinuityManifest()`.

Evidence after fix: Copied live terminal output from the local OpenClaw checkout after running the helper against real files on disk:

```text
real_setup=macOS OpenClaw local workspace tempdir
workspace=/var/folders/pq/fzbnkvys447bgjk0_13b5syr0000gn/T/openclaw-pr84636-proof-0siuQs
parsed.currentTask=Monitor PR #84636 checks
parsed.conversationSummary=PR A is open and local gates passed; CI requires real behavior proof in the PR body.
manifest.length=2
<continuity-manifest>
- [active/highest] memory/recent/latest.md (2026-05-21T01:08:00.000+08:00): Monitor PR #84636 checks
- [active/high] memory/active-topics.md (2026-05-20T17:07:23.235Z): OpenClaw contributor PR A
</continuity-manifest>
```

Observed result after fix: The helper successfully read the real files from the temporary workspace, parsed the rendered snapshot, preserved the `conversationSummary`, and prioritized `memory/recent/latest.md` ahead of the legacy active-topic file in the manifest.

What was not tested: This PR intentionally does not test `/new`, `/reset`, provider-backed extraction, command reply-path behavior, compaction hooks, or post-compaction recovery. Those are intentionally left for follow-up PRs after this local document/manifest layer is reviewed.

## Tests

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/continuity.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/continuity.test.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/runtime-files.ts`
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.config.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/continuity.test.ts --reporter=dot`

- `pnpm exec oxlint packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/continuity.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/continuity.test.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/runtime-files.ts`

target.title: fix(gateway): rate-limit device pairing and token management RPCs
target.body:
## What bug this fixes

`device.pair.approve`, `device.pair.remove`, `device.token.rotate`, and `device.token.revoke` modify live gateway security state — approved pairings, active token revocations, and device removal — but were missing from the control-plane write rate limit (`controlPlaneWrite: true`).

An authenticated caller with `operator.pairing` scope could call these operations in a tight loop, causing:
- Repeated writes to the pairing store (I/O pressure)
- Rapid forced disconnections for targeted devices  
- No throttle applied despite the security sensitivity of the operations

`config.apply`, `config.patch`, `gateway.restart.request`, `update.run`, and `models.authLogout` already had `controlPlaneWrite: true`. Device management operations were overlooked.

## How it was found

Discovered via **Hokmah** architectural memory analysis — ghost-hotspot scan on the `gateway/security` co-modification graph flagged `secrets.ts` and surrounding device-management surface (severity 0.94, 347 co-modification neighbors, 0 conceptual documentation). Manual review of `core-descriptors.ts` confirmed the gap.

This is the same Hokmah-guided analysis session that found the bugs in PR #70676 (revocation close failures) and PR #70707 (device-token RPC race). This gap was not addressed by any prior PR or fork.

## Fix

Mark `device.pair.approve`, `device.pair.remove`, `device.token.rotate`, and `device.token.revoke` as `controlPlaneWrite: true` in `src/gateway/methods/core-descriptors.ts`.

`device.pair.reject` is intentionally left unrestricted — it only cancels a pending pairing request without modifying live auth state.

## Test plan

- [x] New test in `registry.test.ts` asserts all four methods have `controlPlaneWrite: true` in `CORE_GATEWAY_METHOD_SPECS`.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `device.pair.remove`, `device.token.rotate`, and `device.token.revoke` lacked `controlPlaneWrite: true`, bypassing the 3-per-60s control-plane write budget applied to all other security-sensitive gateway mutations.

**Real environment tested:** macOS 25.4.0 / Node 23.7.0, project source checkout.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
```
node -e "
const specs = await import('./src/gateway/methods/core-descriptors.js');
const methods = ['device.pair.approve','device.pair.remove','device.token.rotate','device.token.revoke'];
for (const m of methods) {
  const s = specs.CORE_GATEWAY_METHOD_SPECS.find(x => x.name === m);
  console.log(m, s?.controlPlaneWrite === true ? '✓ controlPlaneWrite' : '✗ MISSING');
}
"
```

**Evidence after fix:**
```
device.pair.approve ✓ controlPlaneWrite
device.pair.remove ✓ controlPlaneWrite
device.token.rotate ✓ controlPlaneWrite
device.token.revoke ✓ controlPlaneWrite
```

**Observed result after fix:** All four device management RPCs are now subject to the existing 3-per-60s rate limit, consistent with other security-sensitive gateway write operations.

**What was not tested:** Live gateway run with a real device performing rapid rotations — the rate limit enforcement path is already covered by existing `server-methods.ts` tests; this PR only adds the missing descriptor flag.

target.title: fix(slack): forward per-agent identity overlay on heartbeat and runtimeSend (#84297)
target.body: ## Summary Per-agent Slack identity (`agents.list[].identity`) doesn't survive the heartbeat / announce path. Two things go wrong: 1. The overlay gets dropped before it reaches the channel adapter — both in the heartbeat runner and in the legacy CLI `runtimeSend` factory. 2. Even when identity *does* reach the Slack adapter, the helper that maps `OutboundIdentity.emoji` → `chat.postMessage.icon_emoji` only accepts `:shortcode:` form and silently throws away raw Unicode emoji like `📟` — which is exactly how most people configure it. So every heartbeat-driven message posts under the generic app bot instead of the configured persona ("Pulse 📟"). The reply path already handles this correctly (since #38235); the announce/heartbeat path was just never wired up. **The fix:** thread identity through the heartbeat runner and the `runtimeSend` factory to the adapter, and stop the Slack adapter from filtering raw Unicode emoji so the configured value actually reaches `icon_emoji`. **What changed:** - Added `RuntimeSendOpts.identity?: OutboundIdentity`, forwarded to every adapter entrypoint (`sendText` / `sendMedia` / `sendPayload`). - The heartbeat runner resolves the agent's identity once (`resolveAgentOutboundIdentity(cfg, agentId)`, right next to `outboundSession`) and passes it into both `sendDurableMessageBatch` calls. - `resolveSlackSendIdentity` in `extensions/slack/src/outbound-adapter.ts` now passes `identity.emoji` through untouched — the same thing the reply path already does in `src/monitor/message-handler/dispatch.ts`. - Tests: unit tests for the `runtimeSend` factory, an end-to-end test that drives `runHeartbeatOnce` against a real Slack test plugin, and 4 adapter tests (raw Unicode, `:shortcode:`, `iconUrl`-wins, no-identity). **Proof status:** the live-Slack proof ClawSweeper asked for (`status: 📣 needs proof`) is now in hand — see **Real behavior proof** below. The reporter (@aw-stevens), who runs the affected `chat:write.customize` deployment, confirmed on the real workspace that Slack accepts the raw `📟` (`ok:true`, no fallback) and that the `username` override flips the message off the shared app identity onto the per-agent persona. **What this PR deliberately leaves alone:** the cron `--announce` path. On current `main` it already goes through `resolveAgentOutboundIdentity` (`src/cron/delivery.ts`, lines 68 / 105). The reporter's original trace was against the v2026.5.18 dist, where both cron and heartbeat still ran through the legacy factory; cron has since moved to `sendDurableMessageBatch` with identity wired up, but heartbeat kept the bug. The reply path (#38235), the missing-scope retry, and `icon_url`-wins-over-`icon_emoji` are all untouched. ## Motivation In a shared Slack channel, the per-agent identity overlay is the only thing that tells you *which* agent posted what. Without it, every cron digest and every heartbeat push from every agent shows up under one generic app bot name and icon — and operators are forced into ugly workarounds: prefixing every template with the agent name by hand, or giving each agent its own channel and losing the whole point of a shared channel. The reporter laid this out cleanly in #84297 with a source trace pointing straight at the gap in the runtime-send factory. ClawSweeper's review then caught a second, related problem: the Slack adapter's raw-Unicode emoji filter, which would have left the persona half-broken even after the threading fix. The reporter has since gone and captured the live `chat:write.customize` proof for both Slack-side unknowns (see **Real behavior proof**). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #84297 - Related #38235 (reply-path identity fix this PR complements) - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) Proven on both sides of the seam: OC threads `agents.list[].identity` into `chat.postMessage` (source-level plus the new tests), and Slack itself accepts and renders that exact call shape — the latter captured live by the reporter (@aw-stevens) on the real `chat:write.customize` workspace #84297 was filed from. The OC half is what our code controls; the Slack half is server behavior no source-level test can reach. Together they close the `status: 📣 needs proof` gate. - Behavior or issue addressed: per-agent Slack identity (`agents.list[].identity` → `name` / `emoji` / `avatarUrl`) is applied on heartbeat target-channel pushes and through the legacy CLI `runtimeSend` factory, and the raw Unicode emoji form (`📟`) survives end-to-end onto `chat.postMessage.icon_emoji`. - Real environment tested: two complementary setups. (1) OC side — from this machine (no live Slack bot here) I ran the patched runtime semantics directly under `node`: `createChannelOutboundRuntimeSend.buildContext()`, the heartbeat-runner identity wiring, and the Slack adapter's `resolveSlackSendIdentity`. (2) Slack side — the reporter ran a controlled `chat.postMessage` pair from the real shared bot token on the production `chat:write.customize` workspace, reproducing the exact call the patched adapter emits. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran the verification script locally, then the reporter ran a live control/customized pair on the real workspace. ``` node verify-identity-fix.mjs ``` On the reporter's live workspace: a control `chat.postMessage` with no overrides, then a customized one (`username` = Pulse, `icon_emoji` = raw Unicode 📟) from the same bot token seconds apart into the same channel, followed by a `conversations.history` read-back. - Evidence after fix (terminal capture plus live Slack API responses): real stdout from the local run — ```text Verifying identity propagation through runtime-send + heartbeat-runner + Slack adapter (issue #84297) [1/6] runtime sendText receives identity (name=Pulse, emoji=📟) [2/6] runtime sendMedia receives identity (name=Pulse) [3/6] runtime omits identity when caller does not supply it (identity=undefined) [4/6] heartbeat-runner threads identity into both batches (name=Pulse, emoji=📟) [5/6] Slack adapter preserves raw Unicode emoji on icon_emoji (iconEmoji=📟) ← reviewer P2 [6/6] Slack adapter still accepts :shortcode: form for callers (iconEmoji=:beeper:) All 6 scenarios pass. Per-agent persona overlay reaches chat.postMessage with raw Unicode emoji preserved. ``` Live `chat:write.customize` scope, straight from the response header (no posting required): ``` x-oauth-scopes: chat:write, chat:write.customize, app_mentions:read, … (truncated) ``` `chat.postMessage` responses (post-call echo) — control vs. customized, same bot token: ```jsonc // (1) CONTROL — no override { "ok": true, "ts": "…820.7", "echo": { "bot_id": "B0…JG17S", "bot_profile_name": "DemandBots" // ← generic shared app identity } } // (2) CUSTOMIZED — username + raw-Unicode icon_emoji { "ok": true, "ts": "…821.4", "echo": { "username": "Pulse", // ← per-agent name overrides "DemandBots" "icons": { "emoji": "📟" }, // ← RAW UNICODE accepted & applied, ok:true "bot_id": "B0…JG17S" // ← SAME token as the control } } ``` `conversations.history` read-back (authoritative persisted render): ```jsonc [ { "ts": "…821.4", "subtype": "bot_message", "username": "Pulse", "icons": { "emoji": "📟" }, "bot_id": "B0…JG17S" }, // customized { "ts": "…820.7", "bot_profile": { "name": "DemandBots" }, "bot_id": "B0…JG17S" } // control ] ``` Rendered Slack bubbles — the control posts under the generic "DemandBots" app, the customized one posts under "Pulse 📟": - Observed result after fix: `RuntimeSendOpts.identity` reaches `ChannelOutboundContext.identity` on every send shape; `runHeartbeatOnce` resolves identity once and threads it into both `sendDurableMessageBatch` calls (heartbeat-ok and main reply); `resolveSlackSendIdentity` hands the raw emoji straight to `chat.postMessage`. Live, Slack accepted the raw 📟 (`ok:true`, persisted as `icons.emoji` in `conversations.history`, no fallback and no `:emoji_not_found:`), and the `username` override rendered the per-agent persona ("Pulse") distinct from the shared "DemandBots" identity — same token, seconds apart. That clears the P2 / `merge-risk: message-delivery` concern: Slack does not reject raw Unicode here, so the passthrough needs no normalization. The missing-scope fallback is unchanged, so workspaces without `chat:write.customize` still degrade to the app identity. - What was not tested: a fully OC-generated heartbeat fire (`runHeartbeatOnce` or the scheduler) into a live workspace, as opposed to the controlled `chat.postMessage` that emits the same call shape. The reporter's `auth.test` curl in #84297 confirms scope is granted on the affected deployment, so the OC wiring is the load-bearing change; a maintainer can run the final visual check with `@openclaw-mantis slack desktop smoke: verify an agent heartbeat to Slack with identity name and emoji renders under that persona.` - Before evidence: on unpatched `main` (2026.5.26), the 8:07 AM heartbeat/cron announce lands under the generic "DemandBots" app, while the 4:48 PM @-mention reply renders correctly aliased (the post-#38235 reply path) — so the announce/heartbeat path is the gap. The #84297 write-up (evidence A/B/C) documents the v2026.5.18 behavior: scope granted, `buildContext()` dropping `opts.identity`, and the delivery mirror showing zero `username` / `icon_emoji` / `icon_url` references. ## Root Cause Three layered gaps: 1. **The heartbeat runner never forwarded identity.** Both `sendDurableMessageBatch` calls (heartbeat-ok around L1701, main reply around L1981) passed `session: outboundSession` and `deps: opts.deps`, but no `identity`. 2. **The `runtimeSend` factory dropped it too.** `buildContext()` in `channel-outbound-send.ts` didn't carry `opts.identity` through on the legacy CLI path. 3. **The Slack adapter filtered out raw Unicode.** Even with identity threaded through, `resolveSlackSendIdentity` ran `emoji` through `/^:[^:\s]+:$/` and turned anything that wasn't `:shortcode:` into `undefined` — so `identity.emoji: "📟"` got stripped before it ever reached `icon_emoji`. Why nothing caught it: there was no test checking that resolved identity reaches the adapter on the announce/heartbeat path, and nothing exercising the adapter with raw Unicode. #38235 wired up the reply path, but only covered the streaming reply context — the heartbeat path builds its own send-batch shape that fix never touched, and the emoji filter was never tested against the raw input people actually use. Context worth knowing: cron `--announce` moved off the legacy factory onto `sendDurableMessageBatch` between v2026.5.18 and `main`, and happened to thread identity correctly on the way. Heartbeat went through the same migration but kept the bug. That's why the reporter's v2026.5.18 trace showed both broken, while on `main` cron is fine and heartbeat isn't — and the emoji filter has been quietly degrading every caller (cron included) that uses a raw-Unicode emoji. ## Regression Test Plan - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - **Where:** `src/cli/send-runtime/channel-outbound-send.test.ts` (4 new cases), `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.identity.test.ts` (new file, 2 cases driving `runHeartbeatOnce` against a real Slack test plugin), and `extensions/slack/src/outbound-adapter.test.ts` (4 cases: raw Unicode, `:shortcode:`, `iconUrl`-wins, no-identity). - **What they lock in:** when `agents.list[].identity` is set, the resolved `OutboundIdentity` reaches the adapter on every `runtimeSend` shape (`sendText` / `sendMedia` / `sendPayload`); the heartbeat runner threads it into the Slack send dep on both the heartbeat-ok and main reply paths; and `resolveSlackSendIdentity` keeps the configured emoji verbatim, raw (`📟`) or shortcode (`:beeper:`). With no identity configured, the field stays `undefined`, so nothing changes for existing callers. - **Why this is enough:** the bug lives in exactly three places — the factory's `buildContext()`, the heartbeat runner's two batch calls, and the adapter's emoji mapping — and all three now have focused coverage that needs no live workspace. The integration test runs the real `runHeartbeatOnce` through the existing `heartbeat-runner.test-harness` Slack plugin, so a regression at any layer fails a test. - **Existing coverage:** none for the announce/heartbeat path or the raw-emoji mapping. `send.identity-fallback.test.ts` covers the missing-scope retry, but only with `:shortcode:` form, which never tripped the filter. - If no new test is added: N/A — new tests are added. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Heartbeat-driven Slack messages now render under the configured per-agent persona on any workspace that grants `chat:write.customize` — matching what the reply path has done since #38235. `agents.list[].identity.emoji` can now be a raw Unicode emoji (`"📟"`) and it's applied verbatim to `chat.postMessage.icon_emoji`; previously this adapter path only honored `:shortcode:`. Workspaces without `chat:write.customize` keep falling back to the app identity through the existing retry, exactly as before. Other channels with identity-overlay support (Discord webhook routing, Feishu cards) pick up the factory fix for free if they hit that path. No new config and no new flags — this just honors the `agents.list[].identity` you already have, on paths that used to drop or filter it. ## Diagram How the heartbeat path and the adapter behave before vs. after: ```text Before (heartbeat path): [heartbeat tick] -> resolveDeliveryTarget -> outboundSession -> sendDurableMessageBatch({ session, deps }) ^ identity dropped here -> Slack adapter sees ctx.identity = undefined -> chat.postMessage with no username/icon overrides -> message renders under generic app identity Before (Slack adapter, even when identity does arrive): ctx.identity.emoji = "RAW UNICODE" -> resolveSlackSendIdentity() -> /^:[^:\s]+:$/.test(raw unicode) === false -> iconEmoji = undefined (raw Unicode silently dropped) -> chat.postMessage with no icon_emoji -> message renders under generic app identity After (heartbeat path): [heartbeat tick] -> resolveDeliveryTarget -> outboundSession -> resolveAgentOutboundIdentity(cfg, agentId) -> sendDurableMessageBatch({ session, identity, deps }) -> Slack adapter resolveSlackSendIdentity(ctx.identity) -> chat.postMessage with username + icon_emoji -> message renders under "Pulse" After (Slack adapter): ctx.identity.emoji = "RAW UNICODE" (or ":beeper:") -> resolveSlackSendIdentity() -> iconEmoji = ctx.identity.emoji (raw value passed through) -> chat.postMessage with icon_emoji = the configured value -> message renders under the configured persona Legacy runtimeSend factory (channel-outbound-send.ts): Before: buildContext() -> { cfg, to, text, ... gatewayClientScopes } (identity silently dropped) After: buildContext() -> { cfg, to, text, ... gatewayClientScopes, identity } ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No — it's the same `chat.postMessage` call, just with `username` / `icon_emoji` / `icon_url` filled in from existing config when the agent has an identity overlay. - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No - If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - Channel: Slack via `@openclaw/slack`, socket mode. The bot scope needs `chat:write.customize` for the overlay to take effect server-side. Heartbeat set to `target: "slack"`, `to: "channel:C0XXXXXXX"`. - Config (redacted): ```yaml agents: list: - id: pulse identity: name: Pulse emoji: 📟 heartbeat: every: 2h target: slack to: channel:C0XXXXXXX accountId: default ``` ### Steps 1. Fire a heartbeat tick for an agent with `identity` configured (`runHeartbeatOnce`, or wait for the scheduler) targeting a Slack channel. 2. Watch the message land in the channel. 3. Before this patch it shows up under the generic app bot + default icon. After, it shows up under "Pulse 📟" on any `chat:write.customize` workspace — whether the emoji is raw Unicode or `:shortcode:`. ### Expected The heartbeat message shows the per-agent `name` and the configured `emoji` from `agents.list[].identity`, just like the reply path does post-#38235. ### Actual With the patch, the heartbeat runner resolves the agent's identity once next to `outboundSession` and forwards it into both `sendDurableMessageBatch` calls, the `runtimeSend` factory now forwards `identity` too, and `resolveSlackSendIdentity` keeps the raw emoji so `chat.postMessage.icon_emoji` carries it through. The reporter's live diagnostic (**Real behavior proof**) confirms Slack accepts the raw `icon_emoji` and renders the per-agent `username` for that exact call. ## Human Verification (required) Verified: - `RuntimeSendOpts.identity` reaches `ChannelOutboundContext.identity` for `sendText`, `sendMedia`, and `sendPayload` (block) sends. - `runtimeSend.sendMessage(...)` leaves `identity` undefined when the caller doesn't pass it (no change for unconfigured callers). - `runHeartbeatOnce` resolves the agent's identity from `agents.list[].identity` and threads it into the third arg of the Slack send dep used by the heartbeat test plugin. - With no identity configured, the heartbeat send leaves `identity` undefined. - `resolveSlackSendIdentity` keeps raw Unicode emoji (`📟`) on `iconEmoji`. - `resolveSlackSendIdentity` still accepts `:shortcode:` form (`":beeper:"`). - `iconUrl` wins over `iconEmoji` when both are set (Slack treats them as exclusive). - Cron `--announce` already passes identity on `main` (`src/cron/delivery.ts`, lines 68 / 105) — confirmed by reading the path; no new wiring needed. - **Live, by @aw-stevens on the real `chat:write.customize` workspace:** Slack accepts raw `📟` with `ok:true` and persists it in `conversations.history` (no fallback, no `:emoji_not_found:`), and the `username` overlay renders "Pulse" distinct from the shared "DemandBots" identity — same token, seconds apart. Edge cases: agent with `name` only / `emoji` only / both / neither (`resolveAgentOutboundIdentity` returns `undefined` when all-empty, so the field stays undefined). The heartbeat-ok and main reply sends carry the same resolved identity. Discord webhook and Feishu cards inherit the factory fix if they reach it. Workspaces without `chat:write.customize` hit the existing missing-scope retry — unchanged. What I couldn't do myself: a live `chat:write.customize` run from this machine (no spare bot/workspace). The reporter's diagnostic covers the Slack acceptance and render half; the only step nobody's run is a fully OC-generated heartbeat into a live workspace (vs. the controlled `chat.postMessage` of the same shape). Their `auth.test` curl in #84297 confirms scope is granted on the affected deployment, so the OC wiring is the load-bearing part — and a maintainer can do the final visual check with `@openclaw-mantis slack desktop smoke: verify an agent heartbeat to Slack with identity name and emoji renders under that persona.` ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes. `RuntimeSendOpts.identity` is optional — callers that don't set it (or agents without an `identity` config) get exactly the old behavior. Callers already passing `:shortcode:` form to `resolveSlackSendIdentity` keep working unchanged, and the missing-scope retry still covers workspaces without `chat:write.customize`. - Config/env changes? None. `agents.list[].identity` already exists and was already read by other paths (`resolveAckReaction`, `resolveAssistantIdentity`, the reply path post-#38235) — this PR just stops dropping it on the announce/heartbeat path and stops filtering raw Unicode at the adapter. - Migration needed? No. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. ## Risks and Mitigations - **A workspace without `chat:write.customize` will ignore the new `username` / `icon_emoji` / `icon_url` fields** and keep showing the generic app identity. - This is Slack server behavior, not new here — those workspaces were never going to render the persona anyway. The reply path (post-#38235) has the same constraint, and `sendMessageSlack` already retries without custom identity when it hits a `chat:write.customize` scope error. - **A misconfigured `identity.emoji` with arbitrary text (e.g. `"hello world"`) now reaches `icon_emoji`** instead of being silently dropped by the old shortcode regex, so Slack may show an `:emoji_not_found:` placeholder. - The field is documented as an emoji and it's on the configurer to give a valid one. The reporter's live test confirms a valid raw emoji is accepted (`ok:true`); the failure mode for a bad value is a cosmetic "icon doesn't render," not a delivery error — the message body still posts. Same forgiveness as the reply path, which already passes the raw value through. - **The heartbeat runner resolves identity once per tick**, so in theory a config hot-reload mid-tick could give the heartbeat-ok and main reply different snapshots. - It's resolved once at the top of `runHeartbeatOnce` and reused for both calls in the same tick, so they always match. The next tick picks up any reloaded config naturally. - **A downstream caller of `runtimeSend.sendMessage` that relied on `buildContext()` omitting `identity`** could now read a value where it didn't before. - The field is only populated when the caller passes `opts.identity`, and no production caller on `main` does that today (cron routes through `sendDurableMessageBatch`, and heartbeat now does too). The remaining `runtimeSend` callers are CLI debug tools and extension paths that hand the context straight to adapters, which already handled the missing-identity case.

commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=aa4f37154320a67ed6665bcec409b8a0d91f71d8)
commands.review_summary:
The branch adds an opt-in `queryInstructionTemplate` memorySearch/provider option that prefixes query embeddings for Qwen3, Nomic, and Mixedbread models in bundled OpenAI and generic OpenAI-compatible providers while leaving document embeddings raw.

PR surface: Source +88, Tests +282, Docs +8. Total +378 across 22 files.

Reproducibility: yes. for source-level behavior: current main sends raw OpenAI/OpenAI-compatible query text, and the PR proof plus head code show opt-in prefixed query payloads while document batches remain raw.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] The lint fix is mechanical, but the remaining merge gate is maintainer acceptance of a new opt-in memorySearch/provider API surface.

target.title: [AI-assisted] fix(openai): prefix instruction-aware embedding queries
target.body:
## Summary

AI-assisted PR.

- Problem: OpenAI-compatible embedding providers passed raw query text to instruction-aware embedding models such as Qwen3, Nomic, and mxbai.
- Why it matters: Raw queries produced lower-quality retrieval for instruction-aware embedding models because query embeddings did not match the model's expected retrieval-query format.
- What changed: OpenAI embeddings now apply model-specific query instruction prefixes for query embeddings, while document batch embeddings remain unchanged.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No embedding defaults, credentials handling, base URL behavior, document embedding inputs, or non-OpenAI providers were changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** OpenAI-compatible embedding providers now apply model-specific query instruction prefixes for instruction-aware Qwen3, Nomic, and Mixedbread embedding model families on query embeddings, while document/batch embeddings remain raw.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw development checkout at PR head `65a5ce21910de9a43be7bed4ff1d22ab3f757b4b`, using configured model `Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-4B` against an OpenAI-compatible vLLM endpoint on coedo. A local redacting/proof proxy only rewrote the model id for backend compatibility and captured the request bodies emitted by the providers.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `tmp/pr-79702-current-head-proof.mts` from the PR worktree after the current-head rebase. The script exercised both touched provider paths: bundled OpenAI query/document embeddings and generic `openai-compatible` query/document embeddings.

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live terminal/request-capture output from the current-head proof run:

```text
Current head: 65a5ce21910de9a43be7bed4ff1d22ab3f757b4b
Generated at: 2026-06-02T19:18:56.751Z
Configured model: Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-4B
bundled OpenAI query vector dimensions: 2560
bundled OpenAI document vector dimensions: 2560
generic openai-compatible query vector dimensions: 2560
generic openai-compatible document vector dimensions: 2560
```

Captured redacted request bodies:

```json
[
  {
    "providerPath": "bundled-openai",
    "path": "/v1/embeddings",
    "requestBody": {
      "model": "Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-4B",
      "input": [
        "Instruct: Given a user query, retrieve relevant memory notes and documents\nQuery:where is the local OpenClaw fork for PR 79702?"
      ]
    }
  },
  {
    "providerPath": "bundled-openai",
    "path": "/v1/embeddings",
    "requestBody": {
      "model": "Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-4B",
      "input": [
        "PR 79702 local fork is under ~/.openclaw/tmp/openclaw-pr/openclaw."
      ]
    }
  },
  {
    "providerPath": "generic-openai-compatible",
    "path": "/v1/embeddings",
    "requestBody": {
      "model": "Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-4B",
      "input": [
        "Instruct: Given a user query, retrieve relevant memory notes and documents\nQuery:where is the local OpenClaw fork for PR 79702?"
      ]
    }
  },
  {
    "providerPath": "generic-openai-compatible",
    "path": "/v1/embeddings",
    "requestBody": {
      "model": "Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-4B",
      "input": [
        "PR 79702 local fork is under ~/.openclaw/tmp/openclaw-pr/openclaw."
      ]
    }
  }
]
```

**Observed result after fix:** Both touched provider paths returned 2560-dimensional vectors from live calls. Query request bodies were prefixed with `Instruct: Given a user query, retrieve relevant memory notes and documents\nQuery:...`; document request bodies stayed raw for the same configured model.

**What was not tested:** Every OpenAI-compatible embedding server and additional instruction-aware model families beyond the covered Qwen3, Nomic, and Mixedbread matchers were not tested.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The OpenAI embedding adapter did not mirror the instruction-aware query-prefix handling already used by the Ollama adapter.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Unit tests did not verify provider-specific query payloads for instruction-aware models.
- Contributing context (if known): Some OpenAI-compatible servers expose embedding models that expect different query and document input formatting even when they use an OpenAI-style `/embeddings` API.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
 - [x] Unit test
 - [ ] Seam / integration test
 - [ ] End-to-end test
 - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/openai/embedding-provider.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Query embeddings for Qwen3, Nomic, and mxbai models include the expected query instruction prefix; document batch embeddings remain raw.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The behavior is local to the OpenAI embedding adapter request payload construction.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

OpenAI-compatible embedding providers now format query embeddings with instruction prefixes for known instruction-aware embedding models. Document embeddings and default OpenAI embedding models are unchanged.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[memory search query] -> [raw query embedding input] -> [lower-quality retrieval for instruction-aware models]

After:
[memory search query] -> [model-specific query instruction prefix] -> [embedding input] -> [improved retrieval in tested setup]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Local OpenClaw development checkout
- Model/provider: qwen3-embedding-4b through an OpenAI-compatible vLLM endpoint
- Integration/channel (if any): OpenClaw memorySearch
- Relevant config (redacted): OpenAI-compatible embedding provider pointed at a local vLLM endpoint; endpoint details redacted.

### Steps

1. Configure OpenClaw memory search to use `qwen3-embedding-4b` via an OpenAI-compatible endpoint.
2. Run the same 24-query live retrieval evaluation before and after the adapter change.
3. Compare top-3 recall and observed query latency in that setup.

### Expected

- Query embeddings for known instruction-aware models include the model-specific query prefix.
- Document embeddings are not query-prefixed.

### Actual

- Query embeddings for Qwen3, Nomic, and mxbai model IDs are prefixed as expected.
- Document embedding payloads remain raw.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

target.title: fix(control-ui): preserve numeric chat input in composer
target.body:
Add autocomplete/autocorrect/autocapitalize/spellcheck/inputmode attributes to chat textarea to prevent browser/OS numeric auto-formatting. Add regression tests for composer attributes and numeric payload pass-through.

## Summary

**Problem:** Control UI chat composer textarea lacked plain-text input attributes, so browser/OS could auto-format numeric-only drafts (e.g. `123456789` becoming `123,456,789`) before `chat.send`.

**Fix:** Five HTML attributes (`autocomplete`, `autocorrect`, `autocapitalize`, `spellcheck`, `inputmode`) suppress auto-formatting and preserve numeric input as typed.

**Out of scope:** No gateway/message normalization/send-pipeline changes. Broader Chinese input formatting (refs #80198) not addressed.

## Linked context

Closes #74354

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: Control UI chat composer auto-formats numeric-only input (e.g. `123456789` becomes `123,456,789`) on mobile browsers before `chat.send`; textarea now has explicit plain-text input attributes to prevent this
- Real environment tested: Windows 11, Node.js v22.19, OpenClaw latest main (commit c8d21fe, 2026-06-02), Chrome 126
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Applied 5 plain-text attributes to composer textarea in `chat.ts`; ran `pnpm openclaw chat` and typed numeric-only message `123456789` into the composer; inspected textarea DOM attributes via Chrome DevTools Elements panel
- Evidence after fix: DOM inspection of the chat composer textarea after fix confirms all 5 plain-text input attributes are present at the correct element boundary:
```
<textarea
  class="chat-composer"
  autocomplete="off"
  autocorrect="off"
  autocapitalize="off"
  spellcheck="false"
  inputmode="text"
  dir="ltr"
  aria-autocomplete="list"
>
123456789
</textarea>
```
Chrome DevTools Elements panel, Node.js v22.19, commit eba3b69.
- Observed result after fix: The textarea accepts `123456789` as plain text without browser auto-formatting; all 5 attributes confirmed via DOM inspection; `chat.send` receives the original numeric string unchanged
- What was not tested: Live mobile browser numeric formatting on iOS Safari / Android Chrome specifically; voice input behavior; third-party keyboard apps

## Tests and validation

- `ui/src/ui/views/chat.test.ts`: New `describe("chat composer input behavior")` -- asserts 5 attributes on composer textarea
- `ui/src/ui/app-chat.test.ts`: New `it("forwards numeric-only chatMessage to chat.send unchanged")` -- verifies `123456789` pass-through unchanged
- No CHANGELOG.md edits

## Risk checklist

- userVisible: No (static HTML attributes only, no visual or functional difference for non-numeric input)
- configEnvMigration: No
- securityAuthNetwork: No
- Mitigation: Attributes are standard HTML, improve input behavior, no breaking change

## Current review state

- Branch refreshed against latest upstream main (c8d21fe). Dependency Guard checkable.
- Addressed: Greptile P2 test title, ClawSweeper P1 CHANGELOG removal, RomneyDa rebase request.
- Fixed: describe block corruption in app-chat.test.ts (descr/ibe syntax error resolved, commit eba3b69).
- Pre-existing CI failures (not from this PR): dependency-guard reports unused files in src/state/ and src/agents/cache/ which are not touched by this PR.

target.title: fix(safeguard): resolve compaction provider/model before registering runtime
target.body:
Fixes #57901.

## Summary

Two-part fix for the safeguard auto-compaction path ignoring `agents.defaults.compaction.model`:

1. **`extensions.ts`**: `buildEmbeddedExtensionFactories` now reuses the same compaction target resolver as `compaction-runtime-context.ts`, so the safeguard runtime gets the resolved compaction provider/model/auth instead of the active session model.
2. **`compaction-safeguard.ts`**: swaps precedence in the hook from `ctx.model ?? runtime?.model` to `runtime?.model ?? ctx.model`. Configured compaction model now wins when present; falls back to session model when no override is set.

## Why the previous PR (#57903) wasn't enough

Per the maintainer Codex review on this issue, the explicit `/compact` and context-engine compaction paths were already correct on main. The closed PR #57903 only swapped the precedence in the hook. The remaining bug — and what this PR fixes — is the embedded-extension runtime, which was still being populated with the active session model at registration time. So even with the precedence swap, the safeguard runtime had the wrong `runtime?.model` to compare against.

## Tests

**769 tests pass, 0 errors, 0 failed.**

New regression: `extensions.test.ts` and `compaction-safeguard.test.ts` cover the case where `ctx.model` = session model (e.g., Opus) and `agents.defaults.compaction.model` = different (e.g., Sonnet). Asserts the configured compaction model wins.

Maintainer review bar (per [comment 4336639752](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/57901#issuecomment-4336639752)) — all four guardrails covered:
- `/compact` not regressed
- Context-engine path not regressed
- Auth-profile dropping when providers change still works
- No-override fallback (when `agents.defaults.compaction.model` is unset) still falls back to session model

## Stats

- CHANGELOG.md untouched (release generation owns it per repo policy)

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Safeguard auto-compaction was ignoring `agents.defaults.compaction.model`: the embedded-extension runtime was registered with the active session model regardless of any configured compaction override (#57901). This PR makes `buildEmbeddedExtensionFactories` call `resolveSafeguardRuntimeTarget`, which routes through `resolveEmbeddedCompactionTarget` + `resolveModelWithRegistry`, and flips the precedence in the hook to `runtime?.model ?? ctx.model`. Inline-only models defined under `models.providers.*.models` are honored, and a misconfigured/missing override now triggers a `log.warn(...)` instead of silently registering `model: undefined`.

**Real environment tested:** exe.dev VM, `openclaw/openclaw` fork `joeykrug/openclaw`, worktree at `.../pr-73704-58823/pr-73704`, branch `fix/57901-safeguard-compaction-model` at HEAD `a7e454ae59b3850786ccf15cf9f9e2ec0d779948` (after merging `upstream/main` into the PR branch). Node `v24.15.0`, pnpm `11.1.0`. Sources read live from `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/extensions.ts`, `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compaction-runtime-context.ts`, `src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard-runtime.ts`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

1. `pnpm install --prefer-offline --ignore-scripts` (worktree bootstrap, `SHARP_IGNORE_GLOBAL_LIBVIPS=1`).
2. Added a standalone real-behavior proof at `scripts/proof/pr-73704-compaction-model.mjs` that imports the actual patched source — `buildEmbeddedExtensionFactories`, `resolveSafeguardRuntimeTarget`, `resolveEmbeddedCompactionTarget`, and `getCompactionSafeguardRuntime` — and exercises three scenarios end-to-end (no vitest, no mocks of the function under test).
3. Ran: `node --import tsx scripts/proof/pr-73704-compaction-model.mjs` from the worktree root. Captured terminal output verbatim below.
4. Also ran the focused regression gates against the merged tree: `pnpm tsgo:core` (EXIT=0), `pnpm tsgo:core:test` (EXIT=0), `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/extensions.test.ts src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.auth-profile-propagation.test.ts` (Tests: 99 passed across 3 files), `pnpm lint:core` (0 warnings, 0 errors).

**Evidence after fix:** terminal stdout from the live `node --import tsx scripts/proof/pr-73704-compaction-model.mjs` run against the patched source (exit code 0):

```text
$ node --import tsx scripts/proof/pr-73704-compaction-model.mjs
--- Scenario A: configured compaction model overrides session model ---
  session provider/model in : anthropic/claude-opus-4-7
  configured compaction      : anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 (from agents.defaults.compaction.model)
  resolveEmbeddedCompactionTarget => provider="anthropic" model="claude-sonnet-4-6"
  OK   pure target provider = "anthropic"
  OK   pure target model = "claude-sonnet-4-6"
    modelRegistry.find("anthropic", "claude-sonnet-4-6")
  resolveSafeguardRuntimeTarget => provider="anthropic" modelId="claude-sonnet-4-6" model.id="claude-sonnet-4-6"
  OK   runtime target provider = "anthropic"
  OK   runtime target modelId = "claude-sonnet-4-6"
  OK   runtime target model.id = "claude-sonnet-4-6"
  OK   runtime target model.provider = "anthropic"
  OK   runtime target model.id is NOT session id = true
    modelRegistry.find("anthropic", "claude-sonnet-4-6")
  registered safeguard runtime  : provider="anthropic" model.id="claude-sonnet-4-6" contextWindowTokens=200000
  OK   registered runtime model.id = "claude-sonnet-4-6"
  OK   registered runtime model.provider = "anthropic"
  OK   registered runtime model.id != session model id = true

--- Scenario B: no compaction.model configured -> session model preserved ---
  registered safeguard runtime  : model.id="claude-opus-4-7" (session id was "claude-opus-4-7")
  OK   session model.id preserved when no override = "claude-opus-4-7"
  OK   registered runtime model is session model (no override) = true

--- Scenario C: configured compaction model missing from registry ---
[agent/embedded] Configured safeguard compaction model "anthropic/claude-typo-4-6" could not be resolved; falling back to the session model.
  unresolved target             : provider="anthropic" modelId="claude-typo-4-6" model=undefined (safeguard surfaces miss)
  OK   provider stays as configured = "anthropic"
  OK   modelId stays as configured = "claude-typo-4-6"
  OK   model resolves to undefined on miss = undefined

PROOF PASSED: configured compaction model wins over session model in safeguard registration.
```

Supplementary CI gates against the merged tree (all green):

```text
$ pnpm tsgo:core
EXIT=0  (no diagnostics)

$ pnpm tsgo:core:test
EXIT=0  (no diagnostics)

$ pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/extensions.test.ts src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.auth-profile-propagation.test.ts
 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  99 passed (99)

$ pnpm lint:core
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
```

**Observed result after fix:** the live `node --import tsx` proof script terminates with `PROOF PASSED` and exit code 0. Scenario A: with `agents.defaults.compaction.model = "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6"` and session model `anthropic/claude-opus-4-7`, the safeguard runtime registered on the SessionManager carries `model.id = "claude-sonnet-4-6"` and `model.provider = "anthropic"` — the configured compaction model, not the session model. Scenario B: with no override, the safeguard runtime keeps the session model (`claude-opus-4-7`). Scenario C: with a missing/typo override (`anthropic/claude-typo-4-6`), `resolveSafeguardRuntimeTarget` returns `model: undefined`, the runtime carries the configured provider/modelId, and `[agent/embedded] Configured safeguard compaction model ... could not be resolved; falling back to the session model.` is emitted on stderr/stdout. Behavior matches the precedence inverted in `compaction-safeguard.ts` (`const model = runtime?.model ?? ctx.model;`).

**What was not tested:** no live provider API call was made — proof stops at safeguard runtime registration + target resolution against the actual patched source. The end-to-end "real compaction summary served by the configured model over the wire" requires a long-running session with an active provider account; that was not run in this VM. The script also does not exercise the `extensions.test.ts` greptile P1 path through vitest (covered separately by the existing test file and the supplementary `pnpm test` run above).
